articles.title: iPadOS 27: Apple is leaving these five iPad models behind, but owners deserve better
articles.excerpt: We’re about 3 weeks out from when Apple unveiled iOS 27 to the world at WWDC26, and it’s still safe to say: this is an incredible update. It’s incredibly performant, and besides some small quirks and…
articles.body: We’re about 3 weeks out from when Apple unveiled iOS 27 to the world at WWDC26, and it’s still safe to say: this is an incredible update. It’s incredibly performant , and besides some small quirks and a little battery drain, it feels much more usable than iOS 26.5. While iOS 27 does support every device that ran iOS 26, the same isn’t true for iPadOS. A number of older iPadOS 26 devices will not be receiving the iPadOS 27 update, which I think is quite a shame – and Apple should mitigate this one way or another.
articles.author: Michael Burkhardt

articles.title: Ayaneo's new horizontal handheld is an even better remake of the Game Boy Micro
articles.excerpt: The Pocket Micro 2 has a larger battery, improved controls and even a 3.5mm headphone jack.
articles.body:
Ayaneo is making waves with a new horizontal handheld device that pays homage to a classic. This new model, known as the Pocket Micro 2, is described as an even better remake of the iconic Game Boy Micro. It aims to capture the spirit of that beloved portable console with modern enhancements for today's gamers.

Among its notable upgrades, the Pocket Micro 2 boasts a larger battery. This improvement suggests longer play sessions, addressing a common desire for portable gaming devices. Users can also look forward to improved controls, promising a more precise and comfortable gaming experience.

Furthermore, Ayaneo has thoughtfully included a 3.5mm headphone jack on the Pocket Micro 2. This feature provides a direct and reliable way for users to connect their preferred wired audio accessories. It's a welcome addition for those who value traditional headphone options.

This new handheld from Ayaneo appears to offer a compelling blend of nostalgic design and contemporary functionality. For readers keen on portable gaming, the Pocket Micro 2 presents an interesting option to consider. Engadget has the full story on this intriguing device.
articles.author: staff@engadget.com (Jackson Chen)

articles.title: I analyzed 500 Upwork proposals with a 95% win rate. Here's the one pattern that decided everything.
articles.excerpt: There's a question every freelancer on Upwork asks at some point: why do some people get replies on almost every bid while most people hear nothing? I've been in the first group for a while now - 95%…
articles.body: There's a question every freelancer on Upwork asks at some point: why do some people get replies on almost every bid while most people hear nothing? I've been in the first group for a while now - 95% bid win rate across hundreds of proposals. I'm not a better writer than most. I don't have more experience. I changed one thing. I stopped writing about myself. The proposal that lost Here's what a losing proposal looks like. You've seen it because you've written it: "Hi! I'm a web developer with 7 years of React experience. I've worked on projects similar to yours and I'm confident I can deliver high-quality results. Here's my process: Review requirements Design architecture Implement and test Deliver and iterate Looking forward to hearing from you!" Everything in that proposal is about you.
articles.author: Yawar Abbas

articles.title: Why I still use NGINX over everything else
articles.excerpt: People keep recommending Caddy. The config is cleaner, automatic HTTPS, three lines and you're done. They're not wrong. For a side project or a small team that doesn't want to think about infrastructu…
articles.body: People keep recommending Caddy. The config is cleaner, automatic HTTPS, three lines and you're done. They're not wrong. For a side project or a small team that doesn't want to think about infrastructure, Caddy is fine. I still use NGINX. Here's why. The setup that made it clear Earlier this year I deployed a production system on a single VPS. Hostinger KVM2, 2 vCPU, 8 GB RAM. The kind of system that sits quiet for months and then gets hit hard in a short window, hundreds of concurrent users at the same moment. The requirement was 1,000 requests per minute. I load tested to 2,500 requests per second. Zero errors. Zero timeouts. p99 on the result lookup was 308ms. No autoscaling. No managed load balancer. No cloud bill. One NGINX config that I wrote by hand. That's the argument.
articles.author: Mishan Raj Shah

articles.title: 📝 Caso de Estudio: Cómo construir un SaaS Multi-tenant con Next.js 16, NestJS 11 y PostgreSQL RLS
articles.excerpt: Introducción: El problema de los portafolios aburridos Seamos honestos. La mayoría de los portafolios de desarrolladores se ven exactamente iguales: una plantilla minimalista genérica, una cuadrícula…
articles.body: Introducción: El problema de los portafolios aburridos Seamos honestos. La mayoría de los portafolios de desarrolladores se ven exactamente iguales: una plantilla minimalista genérica, una cuadrícula con proyectos simples y una lista de tecnologías que nadie entra a auditar. Cuando rediseñé mi espacio web, quise enfocarlo de otra manera: mostrar ingeniería real aplicada a producción. En lugar de subir 10 proyectos pequeños, decidí desglosar casos de estudio profundos. Este es el análisis técnico de Äbasto, un SaaS B2B full-stack para la gestión de inventario y puntos de venta (POS) que resuelve el aislamiento de datos, subdominios dinámicos y un sistema de suscripción con periodos de gracia bajo un único monorepo con pnpm workspaces .

articles.title: Star Fox has me nervous about Switch 2’s Zelda: Ocarina of Time remake
articles.excerpt: Star Fox is yet another fine, but safe remake for Nintendo, but Ocarina of Time deserves something more daring.
articles.body: Nintendo has a monumental task in front of it. Later this year, it will try to iterate on perfection with a full remake of The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time . Considering that the Nintendo 64 classic is still regularly cited as the single greatest video game of all time, the stakes are higher than your average double dip. Even a short teaser that showed nothing more than a slumbering Young Link was enough to drum up discontent among fans.

articles.title: The Future of SEO Has Nothing to Do With Search
articles.excerpt: Or: how I learned a machine might introduce us before my website ever does. Every few years, the internet reinvents discovery. Directories gave way to search engines. Search engines gave way to social…
articles.body: Or: how I learned a machine might introduce us before my website ever does. Every few years, the internet reinvents discovery. Directories gave way to search engines. Search engines gave way to social feeds. Social feeds gave way to recommendation engines. Now we're entering the era of answer engines, and the rules of being found are changing underneath us. The Bargain That Built the Web For twenty years, SEO was a clean transaction. Create content. Help a crawler understand it. Rank for the right keywords. Receive traffic. First place won. Tenth place lost. Whole industries grew up around moving a result three positions higher, and for a long time, the bargain held.
articles.author: Ken W Alger

articles.title: Anthropic, Google, and Microsoft just built a shared security team for open source. AI is why.
articles.excerpt: AI can now scan major open-source projects and surface a batch of real, exploitable vulnerabilities in a single pass. That's a defensive win — until you remember attackers have the same tools. Anthrop…
articles.body: AI can now scan major open-source projects and surface a batch of real, exploitable vulnerabilities in a single pass. That's a defensive win — until you remember attackers have the same tools. Anthropic, Google, Microsoft, OpenAI, AWS, and 15 other organizations aren't waiting for that race to get worse. On Thursday they launched Akrites under the Linux Foundation — a coordinated body built specifically for AI-era vulnerability discovery, remediation, and disclosure in critical open-source software.
articles.author: Andrew Kew

articles.title: 60 Themes, 51 Components, still 0 Dependencies. Yumekit v0.5 Released!
articles.excerpt: Back in May we here at Waggy Labs launched the beta release of our Web Component UI kit "Yumekit". Yumekit is a pure web component UI toolkit. Upon its release, it was comprised of roughly 36 fully st…
articles.body: Back in May we here at Waggy Labs launched the beta release of our Web Component UI kit " Yumekit ". Yumekit is a pure web component UI toolkit. Upon its release, it was comprised of roughly 36 fully styled and fully functional UI components that work with just about every web architecture straight out of the box. No configuration or setup necessary, all one needs to do is include the Yumekit script (using either a CDN or installed through NPM) and start building. All components come styled out of the box with no need to include any style sheets. Last week, we launched version 0.5.
articles.author: Jeff Rodgers

articles.title: Introducing Siyarix v1.0.0 — An Open-Source AI-Powered Cybersecurity Orchestration Framework
articles.excerpt: Today I'm excited to announce the first stable release of Siyarix (v1.0.0)! Siyarix is an open-source AI-powered cybersecurity orchestration framework that combines AI with existing cybersecurity tool…
articles.body: Today I'm excited to announce the first stable release of Siyarix (v1.0.0)! Siyarix is an open-source AI-powered cybersecurity orchestration framework that combines AI with existing cybersecurity tools through a unified command-line interface. The goal is to simplify security workflows, improve automation, and provide an extensible platform for security professionals and researchers. Current Highlights • AI-powered workflow orchestration • Natural-language commands • Multiple AI provider support • Plugin system • CLI-first architecture • Cross-platform support This is only the first stable release, and I'm actively looking for: • Feedback • Feature suggestions • Bug reports • Documentation improvements • Contributors Project Links 🌐 Website https://siyarix.github.io 💻 GitHub https://gith…

articles.title: Grand Theft Auto 6 Reportedly Won’t Get A Disc-Version Even Months After Launch, Despite Promising New Email From Rockstar
articles.excerpt: A new article from The Hollywood Reporter says that while the support email was real, its language was clunky and misunderstood
articles.body:
The future of physical game releases for highly anticipated titles like Grand Theft Auto 6 has been a point of discussion among players. Recent reports suggested that fans might not see a disc version of GTA 6 available, even for several months following its digital launch. This news naturally sparked considerable debate within the gaming community.

A glimmer of hope appeared when a new email from Rockstar began circulating, which many interpreted as a promising sign for a potential physical release. This communication seemed to suggest that a disc version could still be on the cards, perhaps arriving sometime after the initial launch window. The prospect of holding a physical copy of such a monumental game was exciting for many.

However, a new article from The Hollywood Reporter has shed further light on the situation, clarifying the intent behind Rockstar's message. It appears the support email was indeed authentic, but its wording proved to be less than clear. The language used was reportedly clunky, leading to widespread misunderstanding among those who read it.

This clarification suggests that the initial reports regarding Grand Theft Auto 6's disc availability likely remain accurate. While the email was real, its misinterpretation means that players should temper expectations for a physical edition, especially in the months immediately following the game's release. For the full story and more details on this development, Kotaku has the complete report.
articles.author: Zack Kotzer

articles.title: This Week In Space podcast: Episode 216 — Dark Matter Intelligence
articles.excerpt: On Episode 216 of This Week In Space, Rod Pyle and Tariq Malik talk with physicist Dr. Daniel Whiteson about other intelligences is beyond our scope.
articles.body: On Episode 216 of This Week In Space , Rod Pyle and Tariq Malik talk with physicist Dr. Daniel Whiteson about other intelligences is beyond our scope. Whiteson, particle physicist at CERN's Large Hadron Collider and professor at UC Irvine, joins us to explore less conventional thoughts on first contact. We look at the challenges of communicating with extraterrestrial intelligences, potential difficulties of communicating via non-terrestrial mathematics and physics, and the possibility that extraterrestrials might actually be terrestrial... but existing as denizens of the dark energy universe. One might be sitting in your lap right now! Join us for the far-ranging discussion. Download or subscribe to this show at: https://twit.tv/shows/this-week-in-space .
articles.author: Space.com Staff

articles.title: Wardogs CEO explains how the upcoming hardcore FPS will respect your time
articles.excerpt: Wardogs' progression systems cater to both hardcore players and those who play more casually, dipping in and out of sessions.
articles.body: Wardogs , the much-anticipated FPS from Bulkhead, is a complex game. Beneath the surface-level gameplay — 100 players duking it out for zone control on a massive map — there's a cash-focused metagame, skill levels, seasonal resets, gold bars, and more. And while die-hard players will be heavily rewarded for investing a lot of time into the game, CEO Joe Brammer is acutely aware that for Wardogs to succeed, it needs to also be approachable for those who can only play casually.
articles.author: Ford James

articles.title: SpaceCamp at 40: A wish-fulfillment fantasy brought down to earth by NASA's real-life disaster
articles.excerpt: After the Challenger tragedy, nobody wanted to see a film about five kids on an adventure in space.
articles.body: When "SpaceCamp" landed in theaters in June 1986, it should have been the ultimate wish-fulfillment movie, as if "The Goonies" had cashed in their hard-earned pirate treasure in return for a spin in an actual Space Shuttle. It boasted a bigger budget than "Top Gun", a cast of up-and-coming young stars — including a future Oscar-winner — and a score by Hollywood's most famous composer. Twentieth Century Fox had bet big on a blockbuster hit, but a real-life tragedy four months earlier ensured the film was doomed to be an also-ran at the box office.
articles.author: Richard Edwards

articles.title: Blue Origin plans to fly New Glenn rocket again this year despite massive rocket explosion (video)
articles.excerpt: After its rocket explosion, Blue Origin wants to complete repairs and put another New Glenn on the launch pad before the end of 2026, according to CEO Dave Limp. That's very ambitious.
articles.body: Blue Origin is hard at work at its Cape Canaveral launch pad, collecting debris and repairing the damage after an explosive accident last month. The company's New Glenn rocket exploded during a fueling test last month at Launch Complex-36 (LC-36). The resulting fireball laid waste to the surrounding facility, and erupted in a burst with a glow visible more than 100 miles (160 kilometers) away. In the aftermath, Blue Origin CEO Dave Limp voiced confidence that, despite the setback, the company would bring New Glenn back to the pad for a launch before the end of the year. He doubled down on that assessment in an X post on Thursday (June 25), which featured a timelapse video of the work done at LC-36 over the past few weeks. "Huge shoutout to the team who have been working 7x24" Limp said.
articles.author: Josh Dinner

articles.title: NASA used a drone to deliver a human kidney. Is this the future of transplant transport?
articles.excerpt: The drone flew beyond line of sight with a kidney not viable for organ transplant, to test the concept for future deliveries to patients.
articles.body: NASA is hoping to use drones to speed up organ delivery for transplant patients. A flight test earlier this month at NASA's Langley Research Center in Virginia saw a drone pick up a kidney and fly it for the first time beyond "line of sight", or the distance from which a drone is visible by an operator. Keeping a line of sight on a drone is a typical requirement for flight safety, but NASA is developing tools that may allow these machines to fly further away from operators in populated environments more regularly. The kidney on the June 5 flight test was not viable for organ transplantation, which is why the agency and partner United Network for Organ Sharing were able to use it, according to WTKR .
articles.author: Elizabeth Howell

articles.title: Strange glowing 'bow-and-arrow' structure may be a giant cosmic shock wave created by a supersonic galaxy collision
articles.excerpt: A bizarre "bow-and-arrow" radio galaxy nearly 1.8 million light-years across could reveal how galaxy clusters sculpt some of the universe's largest structures.
articles.body: A bizarre radio galaxy discovered by a citizen scientist has left astronomers puzzled, revealing a never-before-seen "bow-and-arrow" structure that could offer rare insight into how galaxies are reshaped by colossal shock waves as they plunge through galaxy clusters. Named RAD-BAARG (short for Radio Bow-And-Arrow Radio Galaxy), the object spans nearly 1.8 million light-years across, making it almost 18 times wider than the Milky Way . Its unusual structure was first identified by a citizen scientist participating in the RAD@home Astronomy Collaboratory, which allows volunteers to review telescope data and flag unusual features that might otherwise be missed. Astronomers say they haven't seen anything like it.

articles.title: FBI Warns Russian Intelligence Hackers Target Signal Backup Recovery Keys
articles.excerpt: The FBI and CISA have updated their March warning about Russian intelligence phishing Signal accounts, and the operators have added a step: they now coax targets into handing over their Signal Backup…
articles.body: The FBI and CISA have updated their March warning about Russian intelligence phishing Signal accounts, and the operators have added a step: they now coax targets into handing over their Signal Backup Recovery Key. Hand it over once, and the attacker can restore the account's backup, read the private and group message history, and take over the account. Worse, the key keeps working.
